title: The Disproportionate-Assets Case: A Continuing Story! (1)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the serialized case history, exact dates and amounts, attributed allegations, procedural distinctions, quoted judicial reasoning, and the source's “To be continued” ending. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which only clear OCR character, malformed combining-mark, spacing, and word-break errors are repaired. The canonical source remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: A Continuing Story! (1)
Udanpirappē,

Jayalalithaa is now governing Tamil Nadu for the third time. In the past, various cases were conducted against her. Although trial courts convicted her in accordance with law in some of them, she later appealed and escaped those convictions. In certain cases from which, despite every effort, she has been unable to escape, she has somehow obtained adjournment after adjournment in a manner without historical precedent, worthy of a place in the *Guinness* records, and has kept postponing judgment. One of the most important of those is the disproportionate-assets case proceeding in Bengaluru.

The people of Tamil Nadu should know in what year this case began and how many adjournments were secured. Today, cases against people accused of corruption involving one or two thousand rupees are completed within a few months and they undergo punishment. Should the people not understand how a person accused of irregularly accumulating assets worth thousands of crores remains Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu and attempts in every way to bend and choke justice? That is why it has become necessary to explain, instalment after instalment, how she has challenged the norms of the administration of justice in this and several other cases.

In January 1991, the DMK government, which commanded a majority, was dismissed by the Union Government on the accusation that it supported the Eelam Tamils. In the election that followed, the AIADMK won. Jayalalithaa came to power in Tamil Nadu for the first time and served as Chief Minister from 1991 to 1996.
According to particulars placed before the Rajya Sabha, Jayalalithaa's assets before she first assumed office as Chief Minister, as of 1 July 1991, were worth Rs 2,01,83,957.
Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu from 1991 until May 1996. After she had enjoyed five years in office, the value of her assets as of 30 April 1996 was Rs 66,65,20,395.

While Jayalalithaa was still Chief Minister, the DMK pointed to the proliferation of large-scale corruption in Tamil Nadu. On 15 April 1995, the General Secretary, the Professor, and I, together with Murasoli Maran, Nanjilar, Arcot Veerasamy, Duraimurugan, N.V.N. Somu and Rahman Khan, presented then Governor Channa Reddy with a 539-page list of corruption charges.
We listed twenty-eight instances of corruption. Charge number twenty-five concerned Jayalalithaa's alleged accumulation of assets beyond her known income. It began:

“Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Selvi Jayalalithaa, Sasikala, her business associate who lives with her in her residence, and Sasikala's brothers have been buying and accumulating a mountain of movable and immovable property throughout Tamil Nadu. Since Selvi Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister, the manner and speed with which those close to her have purchased property have shocked the public and made people wonder how they are able to do it.”

We then set out the details of Jayalalithaa's accumulation of assets published in the 15 April 1995 issue of *India Today*. The people of Tamil Nadu and India already knew this report well. It was not something we invented and circulated.

Before us, Subramanian Swamy had sought the Governor's sanction to prosecute Chief Minister Jayalalithaa on corruption charges. The Governor granted him sanction by letter. Angered by this, AIADMK members created a disturbance at a Janata Party public meeting in Chennai on 6 April 1995, throwing stones and footwear at Subramanian Swamy and Chandralekha. Because the Governor had authorised prosecution, the AIADMK government even adopted a resolution in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly on 27 April 1995 demanding Governor Channa Reddy's recall.

In a petition filed before the Principal Sessions Court in Chennai on 14 June 1996, Dr Subramanian Swamy alleged that Jayalalithaa, while Chief Minister between 1 July 1991 and 30 April 1996, had accumulated assets disproportionate to her income. The court accepted the petition and ordered an investigation. Lathika Saran, then a police officer, directed police officer V.C. Perumal on 26 June 1996 to inquire into the complaint.

Jayalalithaa approached the High Court to stop the investigation. Although it was stayed for a short time, the High Court later lifted the stay and permitted the investigation to continue. Jayalalithaa then sought anticipatory bail from the High Court. Rejecting it, the Court held that there was prima facie material underlying the corruption case against her and that it was incorrect to say the case had been filed because of political hostility.

Senior police officer V.C. Perumal completed the preliminary inquiry. Finding preliminary evidence supporting the allegations, he ordered a detailed investigation and appointed Nallama Naidu as the investigating officer on 7 September 1996. Nallama Naidu registered the First Information Report on 18 September. Sixteen police inspectors were assigned to assist him, and the investigation proceeded. The accused's bank accounts were frozen and they were summoned for inquiry.

On 16 October 1996, the Principal Sessions Court granted permission to inspect in person the immovable properties Jayalalithaa had purchased in various parts of Tamil Nadu and gather the necessary evidence. On 6 December 1996, the court also authorised searches of Jayalalithaa's Chennai residence and Hyderabad vineyard, and the searches were carried out.

At that time, Jayalalithaa had been arrested in another case and was in prison. Investigating officer Nallama Naidu met her there and obtained her consent to appoint Bhaskaran and Vijayan as her representatives during the search of her residence. From 7 to 12 December 1996, police searched her home and Hyderabad vineyard. Gold and diamond jewellery, silver articles, documents and cash found in the house were seized. Everything except the silver articles was placed in the court's custody. The silver articles were left in the house under the responsibility of a man named Bhaskaran. After submitting the investigation report, the police filed the charge sheet before the Chennai Special Court on 4 June 1997.

The following day, 5 June 1997, summonses were issued to the accused, Jayalalithaa, Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi. On 21 October 1997, the Special Court dismissed the discharge petitions filed by accused numbers two, three and four, Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi, and framed charges against all four accused. There were 259 witnesses. All but thirty-nine were examined.

Jayalalithaa's side challenged in the Madras High Court the creation of the Special Court and appointment of a special judge. Since the trial arrangements had been made in accordance with the Constitution of India, their case was dismissed. Their appeal to the Supreme Court was also dismissed on 14 May 1999 by Justices G.T. Nanavati and S.P. Kurdukar. It was thus established that creating a Special Court and appointing a special judge to try Jayalalithaa and the other accused were constitutionally valid.

Tamil Nadu saw another change of government in May 2001. The first accused in the case assumed office as Chief Minister. After Jayalalithaa's government returned in 2001, the trial slowed. Following the Supreme Court's ruling that her assumption of office was invalid, she stepped down as Chief Minister on 21 September 2001. Other convictions against her were subsequently set aside on appeal during the AIADMK administration. She contested and won a by-election and returned as Chief Minister on 2 March 2002.

The case against Jayalalithaa and three others resumed in November 2002 before Judge R. Rajamanickam in Chennai's First Special Court, while the AIADMK was in power. It was decided to recall seventy-six witnesses who had already testified, and their re-examination proceeded from November 2002 to 21 February 2003. The seventy-six witnesses who had previously given evidence in the disproportionate-assets case were recalled under the AIADMK administration. They gave fresh testimony differing from their earlier statements.

Thereafter, under Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, instead of requiring the accused to appear in court, questions were taken to them personally and their statements recorded under the court's order. The Public Prosecutor, who ought to have objected to this irregular departure from procedure, raised no objection because he had been appointed by the AIADMK government.

It was in these circumstances that DMK General Secretary Professor Anbazhagan petitioned the Supreme Court to transfer the case to another State. He asked that the case be moved so that the trial against Jayalalithaa could proceed fairly. He said that 250 witnesses had already been examined and the prosecution evidence had ended in 2000, but that witnesses were being recalled after Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister; that during this re-examination several witnesses were changing their earlier testimony; that because the Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption was under the Chief Minister's control, its investigating officers could not be expected to act fairly; and that those officers might disrupt the evidence and attempt to free Jayalalithaa and the other persons connected with the case. He therefore sought an order for an independent body outside Tamil Nadu to conduct the proceedings.

Accepting the Professor's request, the Supreme Court issued an interim stay on 28 February 2003 on the proceedings before the Chennai Special Court. After hearing the petition, the Supreme Court ordered on 18 November 2003 that the case be transferred to Bengaluru.
The judgment delivered by Supreme Court Justices S.N. Variava and H.K. Sema was highly significant. Its important portions are set out here. At the very beginning, the judges said:

“Counsel appearing for Jayalalithaa argued that the petitioner, the Professor, had approached the Supreme Court as a political opponent and that the case had been filed for political reasons. This argument cannot be accepted. In a democracy, opposition parties occupy an important place both within and outside the legislature. They carry the vital responsibility of keeping watch over those in power. Their principal weapon is to oppose the wrongful practices and actions of the ruling party. It is generally they who give voice to the people's grievances.

“In that position, as a member of the Opposition, the petitioner, the Professor, has a genuine concern for both the administration of the State and the administration of justice. A petition filed by such a person cannot be dismissed as one brought for political reasons. In our view, the petitioner has raised many justifiable and acceptable grounds creating strong apprehensions that justice may be denied or administered with bias in this case.”
The Supreme Court stated this in its judgment of 18 November 2003.
(To be continued)
With affection,
M.K.
